request.question: What island that Zanthoxylum hawaiiense can be found on is nicknamed“The Friendly Isle”?

title: Chelerythrine
Nitidine
Zanthoxylum psammophilum
Molokai
List of islands in Isle Royale National Park
Little Deer Isle, Maine
Zanthoxylum gilletii
Fagaronine
Zanthoxylum hawaiiense
Molokai Island Times
sentences: Chelerythrine is a benzophenanthridine alkaloid present in the plant "Chelidonium majus" (greater celandine).
It is a potent, selective, and cell-permeable protein kinase C inhibitor "in vitro".
And an efficacious antagonist of G-protein-coupled CB1 receptors.
It is also found in the plants "Zanthoxylum clava-herculis" and "Zanthoxylum rhoifolium", exhibiting antibacterial activity against "Staphylococcus aureus" and other human pathogens.
Nitidine is a benzophenanthridine alkaloid found in species of the genus "Zanthoxylum ", notably in "Zanthoxylum nitidum".
This compound has an anti-malarial activity.
Zanthoxylum psammophilum (synonym Fagara psammophila Aké Assi) is a species of shrub or small tree in the Rutaceae family.
It is a large liana endemic to Côte d'Ivoire, although in 2005 it was found in Liberia as well.

"Zanthoxylum psammophilum", a new combination created in 1975 to subsume the genus "Fagara" into the genus "Zanthoxylum" based on morphology and secondary metabolites, is the preferred name according to the Conservatoire et Jardin botaniques de la Ville de Genève which has a section that specializes in the conservation and biodiversity of flowering plants of Côte d'Ivoire.
However, the International Plant Names Index lists "Fagara" as the genus, and "Zanthoxylum" as the synonym.
The type specimen for the plant was collected in the ecotone between the lowland Eastern Guinean forests (tropical rainforest) and the inland Guinean forest-savanna mosaic in the Lagunes District.
Molokai ( ; Hawaiian: ] ), nicknamed “The Friendly Isle” is the fifth largest island of eight major islands that make up the Hawaiian Island Chain in the middle of the Pacific Ocean.

It is 38 by 10 miles (61 by 16 km) in size at its extreme length and width with a usable land area of 260 sqmi , making it the fifth-largest of the main Hawaiian Islands and the 27th largest island in the United States.
It lies east of Oahu across the 25-mile (40 km) wide Kaiwi Channel and north of Lānai, separated from it by the Kalohi Channel.
The following is a list of islands in Isle Royale National Park.
Located in Lake Superior, Isle Royale is the second largest island in the Great Lakes system, after only Manitoulin Island in Lake Huron.
At 206.7 mi² (535.4 km²), Isle Royale is the third largest island in the contiguous United States after Long Island and Padre Island.
The island and the surrounding islands and waters make up Isle Royale National Park for a total area of 209 sq mi² (542 km²) of above-water territory.
Little Deer Isle is an island in Penobscot Bay, lying just north of the town (and island) of Deer Isle, of which it is a part.
The island is served by Maine State Route 15 via the Deer Isle Bridge.
The north end of the island is called Eggemoggin and there is a small island with a lighthouse on it at the tip called Pumpkin Island.
Little Deer is about 4 miles long and connects to Deer Isle by way of a causeway.
There are only about 300 year-round residents of Little Deer, but many more summer residents.
Zanthoxylum gilletii, the East African satin wood, is a tree species in the genus "Zanthoxylum" found in Africa.
The fruits are used to produce the spice uzazi.
Fagaronine is a benzophenanthridine alkaloid found in "Zanthoxylum zanthoxyloides" and other species in the "Zanthoxylum" genus.
Zanthoxylum hawaiiense, commonly known Aʻ e or Hawaiʻ i pricklyash, is a species of flowering plant in the citrus family, Rutaceae, that is endemic to Hawaii.
It can be found at elevations of 550 – in dry forests, where it grows on lava flows, and mixed mesic forests on the Island of Hawaiʻ i, Maui, Molokaʻ i, and Lānaʻ i.
It is threatened by habitat loss.
The Molokai Island Times was one of three newspapers on the Hawaiian island of Moloka'i.

It was founded in December 2004 by Brennan Purtzer and Darrell Williams with a subsidy from computer software guru John McAfee, Williams' English cousin, and served as the newest community paper for "The Friendly Isle" of Molokai from 2005–2006, before becoming known as The Molokai Times in January 2007.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: What island that Zanthoxylum hawaiiense can be found on is nicknamed“The Friendly Isle”?
hop1_docs: Zanthoxylum hawaiiense | Zanthoxylum hawaiiense, commonly known Aʻ e or Hawaiʻ i pricklyash, is a species of flowering plant in the citrus family, Rutaceae, that is endemic to Hawaii.  It can be found at elevations of 550 – in dry forests, where it grows on lava flows, and mixed mesic forests on the Island of Hawaiʻ i, Maui, Molokaʻ i, and Lānaʻ i.  It is threatened by habitat loss.

Zanthoxylum thomasianum | Zanthoxylum thomasianum, also known as the St. Thomas prickly-ash, is a species of plant in the Rutaceae family.  It is found in Puerto Rico, the British Virgin Islands, and the United States Virgin Islands.  Its natural habitats are tropical and subtropical dry broadleaf forests and shrublands.  It is threatened by habitat loss, and is the only on St. John listed as "endangered".

Lighthouses on the Isle of Arran | Three lighthouses can be found around the shores of the island of Arran in the Firth of Clyde.  One is located on the Isle of Pladda in the South and the other two can be found on Holy Isle in Lamlash Bay to the East of Arran.  All three are still in service and maintained by the Northern Lighthouse Board.  They are now fully automatic since the electrification of both lighthouses on Holy Isle in 1977.

Zanthoxylum kauaense | Zanthoxylum kauaense, commonly known as Aʻ e or Kauaʻ i pricklyash, is a species of flowering plant in the citrus family, Rutaceae, that is endemic to Hawaii.  It usually inhabits mixed mesic forests at elevations of 300 - , but can also be found in dry and wet forests.  It is threatened by habitat loss.

Zanthoxylum coreanum | The Korean lime tree, Zanthoxylum coreanum Nakai, is a rare shrub that only grows in Korea and China.  In Korea it is called wang-cho-pi tree and is used for many purposes.  There are researches that are done to figure out what compounds are in the "Zanthoxylum coreanum" Nakai and the uses of this plant.

Molokai | Molokai ( ; Hawaiian: ] ), nicknamed “The Friendly Isle” is the fifth largest island of eight major islands that make up the Hawaiian Island Chain in the middle of the Pacific Ocean.  It is 38 by 10 miles (61 by 16 km) in size at its extreme length and width with a usable land area of 260 sqmi , making it the fifth-largest of the main Hawaiian Islands and the 27th largest island in the United States.  It lies east of Oahu across the 25-mile (40 km) wide Kaiwi Channel and north of Lānai, separated from it by the Kalohi Channel.

Shaky Isles | The Shaky Isles or Shaky Islands is a nickname for New Zealand.  At one time this nickname was used in New Zealand itself, though its usage there is now seen as dated; it is still fairly widely used in Australia.

response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Zanthoxylum hawaiiense distribution; Molokai / Molokaʻi nickname

- Zanthoxylum hawaiiense — "can be found ... on the Island of Hawaiʻ i, Maui, Molokaʻ i, and Lānaʻ i." (passage [1]).
- Molokai — "nicknamed “The Friendly Isle”" (passage [6]).
- Ambiguity: spelling variant exists — passage [1] uses "Molokaʻi" (with ʻ) while passage [6] uses "Molokai"; these are the same island name variant (no alternate island candidates share that nickname in the passages).
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority):
  1. No additional retrieval needed — passages already show Z. hawaiiense includes "Molokaʻi" and Molokai is "nicknamed 'The Friendly Isle'"; proceed to produce final answer.

It is placed in the genus Aspidodiadema and lives in the sea.  Aspidodiadema hawaiiense was first scientifically described in 1939 by Ole Theodor Jensen Mortensen, a Danish scientist.
